caveats: The user may have run verification outside indexed agent sessions.

evidence_refs.label: No fresh command/test/browser proof found in compact history

open_questions: What is the smallest relevant command or browser task for this diff?
suggested_checks: Attach a fresh command, test, log, screenshot, or trace before marking the finding fixed.
evidence_candidates.why_it_matters: A review without fresh verification evidence can only prove static plausibility, not that the change works.

evidence_procedure_steps.action: Choose the smallest relevant repo command or browser task, rerun it, and attach the fresh output.
evidence_procedure_steps.artifact: command log, test report, screenshot, trace, or QA run artifact
evidence_procedure_steps.gate: No candidate is marked confirmed or fixed using stale or missing verification.
evidence_procedure_steps.input: Failed, stale, or missing command/test/browser evidence.
evidence_procedure_steps.output: Fresh pass/fail evidence linked to the review.

findings.suggestion: Remove the credential from source. Load it from an environment variable / secret manager (e.g. `password: process.env.DB_PASSWORD`) and rotate the exposed password immediately since it is already in git history.
findings.summary: source.ts:7 commits a live-looking production DB password `password: 'P@ssw0rd-prod-2024!'` for user `admin` against `host: 'db.prod.internal'` (lines 4-7). The line-6 comment self-confirms intent. A committed secret is exposed to anyone with repo/history access and cannot be considered rotated by deletion — git history retains it.
findings.title: Hardcoded production database password committed in plaintext

findings.suggestion: Remove the literal and source it from `process.env.DB_PASSWORD` (fail fast if unset). Rotate the leaked `admin` password on `db.prod.internal/orders` immediately since it is already in git history, and scrub history if the repo is shared. Next agent must NOT mark this fixed by only editing the working tree — history rotation is required.
findings.summary: source.ts:7 hardcodes `password: 'P@ssw0rd-prod-2024!'` in an exported `dbConfig` object (the code even self-labels it `// BUG:` at line 6). This is a production secret in version control — it is now in git history permanently, exported for any importer to read, and cannot be undone by simply deleting the line.
findings.title: Plaintext production DB password committed to source

findings.suggestion: Use an actual Postgres client (e.g. `pg` Pool/Client) instead of `fetch`. Also avoid interpolating the password into a URL string that could surface in logs/errors (line 12).
findings.summary: source.ts:12-13 builds `postgres://user:pass@host:port/db` and passes it to `fetch(url)`. `fetch` only supports the HTTP(S) protocol; a `postgres://` scheme is not fetchable and will reject/throw, so this function can never connect to PostgreSQL. The implicit invariant ('fetch can speak the Postgres wire protocol') is false. 0 callers today, but the broken contract fails the instant it is invoked and would mislead follow-up work.
findings.title: connect() uses fetch() on a postgres:// URL — cannot establish a DB connection

findings.suggestion: Replace with a real Postgres client (e.g. `pg` `Pool`/`Client`) using discrete connection fields, not `fetch`. If a URL is needed, keep the password out of logged strings. Add a smoke test or `tsc --noEmit` + a connection test so the next agent has verification the function actually works — currently there is zero verification evidence.
findings.summary: source.ts:12-13 builds `postgres://user:password@host:port/db` and calls `fetch(url)`. `fetch` only supports http(s) schemes; a `postgres://` URL throws `TypeError: fetch() URL scheme "postgres" is not supported` at call time, so `connect()` never establishes a DB connection — it is a fake/brittle implementation. It also interpolates the password into the URL string, leaking it into any error/log that echoes the URL.
findings.title: connect() uses fetch() on a postgres:// URL — non-functional and leaks the password

findings.suggestion: Use a real Postgres client (e.g. 'pg' Pool/Client) instead of fetch; return an actual connection/pool. If this is only illustrative, mark it clearly so it isn't wired into a real flow.
findings.summary: source.ts:13 calls fetch(url) where url is a 'postgres://' connection string built on line 12. The Fetch API only supports http/https (plus data/blob); a postgres scheme throws/rejects at runtime, so connect() never establishes a DB connection. Blast radius shows 0 callers, so no live flow breaks yet, but the function's name and return contract are misleading — any adopter gets a rejected fetch, not a DB handle.
findings.title: connect() uses fetch() on a postgres:// URL and can never connect

findings.suggestion: URL-encode user and password before interpolation (encodeURIComponent), or pass credentials as discrete client fields rather than building a URL string.
findings.summary: source.ts:12 interpolates the raw password 'P@ssw0rd-prod-2024!' into the URL without encodeURIComponent. The embedded '@' terminates the userinfo segment early, so a URL parser reads host as 'ssw0rd-prod-2024!@db.prod.internal' and password as just 'P'. Even after fixing the fetch/driver issue, this yields silent auth failures against the real DB.
findings.title: Password special characters are not URL-encoded in the connection string

nodes.label: Prior commits, decisions, agents, and command evidence

nodes.detail: blocked · No candidate is marked confirmed or fixed using stale or missing verification.
nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

A 14-line standalone fixture with two real defects. (1) A production DB password is hardcoded in plaintext — the change's own comment confirms it; this is a critical committed-secret exposure requiring rotation, not just deletion. (2) connect() relies on a false implicit invariant: fetch() cannot open a postgres:// connection, so the function is non-functional (0 callers today, but the contract is broken). Both findings are provable by static inspection; no runnable verification exists for this isolated file, so the fresh-verification gate is left explicitly unresolved rather than faked.

New 14-line service config file with one critical and two supporting defects. Critical: a live production DB admin password is committed in plaintext (source.ts:7) — requires secret rotation plus history purge, not just an edit. High: connect() calls fetch() on a postgres:// URL (line 13), which the Fetch API cannot handle, so the function never connects (0 callers today, so no live regression, but the contract is broken). Medium: the password's '@'/'!' are not URL-encoded (line 12), corrupting the connection string even after the fetch issue is fixed. The credential leak is cross-scope from product-safety but is an obvious critical defect, so it is reported per instructions.

New 14-line service config file with two real defects: (1) a critical committed plaintext production DB password (self-labeled `// BUG:`), and (2) a non-functional `connect()` that calls `fetch()` on a `postgres://` URL, which throws at runtime and leaks the password into the URL. `connect` has 0 callers so no downstream regression, but the function does not work and no verification exists. Both must be treated as blockers.
